metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC1 — e.g., `grep -i 'incomplete verdict' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md` and `grep -i 'every.*criterion' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md` — so the prompt-text change has a machine-checkable assertion matching the specific required phrases.
findings.finding: AC1 (the prompt change to completion_verifier.md) has no concrete proving command. The plan specifies that the Verdict & findings section must contain specific language ('requires per-criterion exhaustive evaluation', 'one finding per unmet criterion on FAIL', 'naming only a subset of unmet criteria is an incomplete verdict') but provides no `grep` or assertion to verify the file contains this language — leaving the proving command implicit.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, first bullet: 'The completion-verifier prompt (completion_verifier.md, Verdict & findings section) requires per-criterion exhaustive evaluation...'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'Proved by: `grep -c "incomplete verdict" src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md` returns >= 1 AND `grep -c "every.*criterion\|each.*criterion" src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md` returns >= 1.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the cardinality contradiction in `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria`: choose either (a) injection — 1.0 iff every expected substring matches a distinct finding (extra findings are fine), or (b) strict bijection — 1.0 iff exactly N findings, each matching a distinct expected substring (no extras). State the chosen rule once, removing the conflicting phrase.
findings.finding: The plan's bijective-match scoring rule contains two conflicting cardinality statements that an implementor cannot reconcile: it simultaneously says ">= N findings, each naming a different unmet criterion" (an injection — extra unmatched findings are acceptable) AND "BIJECTIVE match — each expected substring is matched... to a DISTINCT finding, no finding reused" (bijection — implies exactly N findings for N substrings). These are contradictory: strict bijection would score 0.0 when the model emits 3 findings against 2 expected substrings, while the >= N framing would score 1.0.
findings.location: What section / ANNOTATION SCHEMA paragraph: 'enumerates_all_unmet_criteria scorer... scores 1.0 iff there is a BIJECTIVE match — each expected substring is matched... to a DISTINCT finding, no finding reused for two substrings' AND '>= N findings, each naming a different unmet criterion'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the conflicting dual statement with a single rule: 'scores 1.0 iff every expected substring matches at least one finding's `criterion` field (case-insensitive), each expected substring matched to a DISTINCT finding (no finding reused for two substrings) — additional findings beyond the N expected ones do not penalize the score.' This chooses injection (the more robust and behaviorally correct rule for an anti-ratchet check).

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (in the scorer contract description and implementation) that `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` also abstains (_NA) when the verdict in the output is not FAIL (or equivalently, when `case.get('expect') not in FIRE_EXPECTS`), consistent with the existing scorer pattern for polarity guarding.
findings.finding: The `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` scorer as specified will produce a false 0.0 (rather than abstaining) if `expected_unmet_criteria` is present on a PASS-expect case, because 0 findings will match N>0 expected substrings without any guard checking the verdict polarity.
findings.location: What section — ANNOTATION SCHEMA / scorer spec; src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_scorers.py (to be authored)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the scorer spec: 'The scorer also abstains if the output verdict is not FAIL (a PASS output trivially has no findings; scoring it 0.0 would be incorrect). Guard: if `_fired(out)` is False and `expected_unmet_criteria` is non-empty, return _NA.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add guidance (in the ANNOTATION SCHEMA section or a note on the CV-two-unmet-criteria case) that `expected_unmet_criteria` substrings should be drawn from the fixture's AC checklist item text verbatim (or a clearly-unique fragment thereof), since the prompt instructs the model to set `criterion` to the requirement 'verbatim or clearly identifying' — this grounds the substring test in text the model is explicitly directed to reproduce.
findings.finding: The bijective-match mechanism relies on case-insensitive substring matching of `expected_unmet_criteria` entries against LLM-generated `criterion` fields, but the plan gives no guidance on how to choose substrings that survive LLM paraphrase — leaving the CV-two-unmet-criteria case fragile to any model that describes the gap in different words.
findings.location: What section — ANNOTATION SCHEMA; src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/completion-verifier.eval.yaml (CV-two-unmet-criteria case to be authored)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback rule to the `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` scorer spec: specify that findings where `criterion` is absent or null are treated as non-matching (equivalent to an empty string for substring purposes) rather than raising.
findings.finding: The `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` scorer's matching logic reads `finding['criterion']` on each finding, but `criterion` is an `additionalProperty` on the completion_verdict finding schema (not a base `$def` property) — it is absent from the synthesized placeholder finding emitted by `reconcile_verdict` (`criterion: '(unspecified)'` is present there, but other callers may omit it) and may be absent from valid findings. The plan gives no fallback for how the scorer should handle findings where `criterion` is absent or null, creating ambiguity about whether to skip, treat as empty string, or raise.
findings.location: What section / ANNOTATION SCHEMA paragraph: 'each expected substring is matched (case-insensitive substring test on a finding's `criterion` field) to a DISTINCT finding'; also src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md line 163 ('criterion': the specific requirement that failed) and tests/interfaces/store/test_completion_verifier.py line 57 ('criterion is NOT in the shared finding $def')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the scorer description: 'A finding with a missing or null `criterion` field is treated as a non-match (never satisfies any expected substring); it does not cause an error.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the CV-two-unmet-criteria case dict must include both `ticket_context` (the AC text with two gaps) and `files` (fixture code that satisfies neither), matching the shape of all existing completion-verifier eval cases.
findings.finding: The CV-two-unmet-criteria case definition omits ticket_context and files — the two fields the eval solver requires to seed its disposable rebar store and fixture repo. Every other completion-verifier eval case carries both. The plan describes the case as having 'a fixture ticket' with 'two independent AC gaps' but never specifies that ticket_context + files must be present in the YAML case dict, leaving an ambiguity that could produce a broken or incomplete eval case.
findings.location: What section — 'add a dataset case CV-two-unmet-criteria (expect: fail) whose fixture ticket carries exactly two independent AC gaps'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, add: 'The CV-two-unmet-criteria case must carry ticket_context (the ticket AC text naming both gaps) and files (fixture code satisfying neither), following the shape of existing completion-verifier eval cases.'

findings.finding: The 'distinct finding per criterion' matching rule for `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` is under-specified as an algorithm. The plan says 'each naming a different unmet criterion' and 'every substring is matched by a different finding', but the bijective-assignment algorithm (ensuring one finding cannot satisfy two criteria simultaneously) is never defined. A naive implementation that independently tests 'is substr1 in any finding.criterion?' and 'is substr2 in any finding.criterion?' would incorrectly score 1.0 when both substrings appear in the same single finding's `criterion` text, missing the anti-ratchet goal.
findings.location: What section — ANNOTATION SCHEMA paragraph: 'scores 1.0 iff every substring is matched by a different finding, else 0.0'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the scorer description: 'Matching is bijective: assign each `expected_unmet_criteria` substring to a distinct finding by index (a finding already matched to one criterion cannot satisfy another). Score 1.0 iff a complete bijective assignment covering all N substrings exists, else 0.0.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a close-gate AC that requires at least one live-LLM eval run of `CV-two-unmet-criteria` to score 1.0 before the ticket closes (or explicitly name a follow-on ticket that gates on this), so the prompt change has end-to-end behavioral proof as part of the acceptance criteria rather than being purely confirmatory post-close.
findings.finding: The close gate never exercises the newly changed prompt behavior via a live LLM run. The plan's own text states a FakeRunner test 'cannot demonstrate the prompt achieves exhaustive evaluation,' yet makes the close gated only on validate_eval_spec + FakeRunner (both offline). The live eval is 'confirmatory' and runs in a separate pipeline after close — meaning the ticket can close green while the primary behavioral change (exhaustive FAIL enumeration in the prompt) is entirely untested end-to-end.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — second bullet; 'CLOSE-GATE vs CONFIRMATORY' section
findings.suggested_fix: Promote the eval run to a soft gate: require the eval pipeline to have produced a passing result for CV-two-unmet-criteria (scorer == 1.0) before the ticket can be marked closed. If CI infrastructure makes this impractical, name an explicit child/follow-on ticket that gates on the live eval result, so the deferred verification is tracked and not forgotten.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name `tests/unit/test_new_eval_specs.py` explicitly as a consumer that runs `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` over `completion-verifier` and checks all deterministic scorer names are registered — confirm the new scorer registration makes this test pass.
findings.finding: tests/unit/test_new_eval_specs.py is an unenumerated consumer: it runs validate_eval_spec(strict=True) over 'completion-verifier' AND iterates all deterministic scorers checking each name is in known_scorer_names(). This test will fail if enumerates_all_unmet_criteria appears in completion-verifier.eval.yaml but is not registered in REGISTRY — yet the plan never names it as a consumer that must stay green.
findings.location: What section / AC item 2 — 'validate_eval_spec(strict=True) passes on the extended spec'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note under AC item 2: 'Offline proof is also exercised by tests/unit/test_new_eval_specs.py (part of make test), which runs validate_eval_spec(strict=True) over completion-verifier and asserts every deterministic scorer name is in known_scorer_names().'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the 'e.g.' in the CV-two-unmet-criteria fixture description with a concrete stated default (e.g., 'an unimplemented function `foo()` referenced in AC1' and 'no test for the error-handling branch specified in AC2') and specify the corresponding `expected_unmet_criteria` substrings, so the executor has a ready-to-use fixture without a design choice.
findings.finding: The `CV-two-unmet-criteria` fixture content is described with 'e.g.' ("e.g. an unimplemented function AND an untested path"), leaving the executor to choose the exact two AC gaps without a stated default. While the intent is clear (any two independent gaps), an executor may wonder whether their specific choice is acceptable, or whether the substrings in `expected_unmet_criteria` need to match the fixture's content in a specific way.
findings.location: What section, paragraph: 'add a dataset case CV-two-unmet-criteria (expect: fail) whose fixture ticket carries exactly two independent AC gaps (e.g. an unimplemented function AND an untested path)'
findings.suggested_fix: State: 'The fixture ticket has AC1 requiring a function `process_result` (not implemented in the fixture diff) and AC2 requiring a unit test for the error path (no test file in the fixture diff). The annotation is `expected_unmet_criteria: ["process_result", "error path"]`.'  This gives the executor a copy-paste-ready fixture and annotation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit statement of the completion-verifier finding field name the scorer reads (e.g., confirm it is `criterion`, or name the actual field), so the scorer implementation and the prompt output spec are mutually consistent.
findings.finding: The scorer specification (What section) says it scores via 'case-insensitive substring test on a finding's `criterion` field', but the prompt-change description never specifies that completion-verifier findings must expose a `criterion` field. If the LLM emits findings whose relevant field is named differently (e.g. `description`, `text`, or `message`), the scorer silently returns 0.0 for every run, making the confirmatory eval always fail without indicating why.
findings.location: What section — scorer annotation schema paragraph ('case-insensitive substring test on a finding\'s `criterion` field') vs. prompt-change bullet ('emit one finding per unmet criterion')
findings.suggested_fix: In the annotation-schema paragraph, add: 'Findings emitted by the completion-verifier are expected to carry a `criterion` field (confirm against the existing finding schema in completion_verifier.md); the scorer reads `finding["criterion"]` for substring matching.' If the field is named differently, update both the scorer spec and the prompt to use the correct name.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move the confirmatory eval-run evidence description out of the numbered Acceptance Criteria into a separate 'Confirmatory evidence (non-gating)' subsection, so AC2 covers only the offline `validate_eval_spec` + scorer registration — items that are actual close gates.
findings.finding: AC2 embeds a 'behavioral proof' item that the plan explicitly marks as NOT a close gate ('it is not a per-PR gate and does not block the close'), yet it appears as a bullet inside the Acceptance Criteria section. This creates a cross-section contradiction: the Acceptance Criteria section conventionally defines what must be true to close the ticket, but this item explicitly cannot block close.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 ('Behavioral proof (confirmatory, in the [eval] eval-CI pipeline — NOT the per-PR close gate)')
findings.suggested_fix: Split AC2 into: (a) a proper AC: 'The `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` scorer is implemented and registered, `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` passes on the extended spec, and `CV-two-unmet-criteria` is present in the eval YAML'; and (b) a separate non-AC note: 'Confirmatory (non-gating, eval-CI pipeline): the live eval run records scorer == 1.0 for CV-two-unmet-criteria.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the `expected_unmet_criteria` annotation-field convention (name, schema, abstain-on-missing behavior) in CLAUDE.md or a scorer-convention docstring/README so future eval spec authors can discover it without reading source code.
findings.finding: The new `expected_unmet_criteria` annotation field establishes an implicit contract between eval spec authors and scorer implementers, but the plan does not mention updating any developer-facing documentation (CLAUDE.md, design doc, or inline docstring convention guide) to record this new field convention. Future spec authors would need to read the scorer source or this ticket to discover the field name, its schema, and the abstain-on-missing behavior.
findings.location: AC: 'ANNOTATION SCHEMA' paragraph; src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_scorers.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add a module-level docstring to eval_scorers.py (or a SCORERS.md) that lists each per-case annotation field consumed by each deterministic scorer, including `expected_unmet_criteria: list[str]` for `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria`, plus the abstain-on-missing policy.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale in the 'What' section explaining why a prompt-level instruction (rather than a post-processing or schema-level change) is the right mechanism — e.g. that it is the only option consistent with the byte-identical reconcile_verdict constraint and keeps the blast radius to a single prompt file.
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for WHY a prompt-level instruction is the right mechanism for forcing exhaustive evaluation, as opposed to structural alternatives. G6 requires the plan to state a positive rationale for the chosen approach.
findings.location: What section / overall plan — no rationale stated
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, prepend: 'A prompt-level instruction is the correct mechanism here because reconcile_verdict must stay byte-identical (it is a public contract seam consumed by gate_ops), ruling out any post-processing aggregation; and because the stop-at-first-failure behavior is a prompt-reasoning failure, not a structural gap in the data flow.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts `scorer_args` is an existing per-case annotation field ('Match the exact annotation-passing mechanism the eval solver already supports'), but this field does not exist anywhere in the eval infrastructure (eval.py, eval_scorers.py, eval_solver.py, completion-verifier.eval.yaml). The plan presents it as pre-existing plumbing to match, but it is actually novel — the implementer must invent and document the convention rather than reuse one.
findings.location: What section — annotation schema paragraph: 'the case carries a `scorer_args` (or the spec's existing per-case annotation field) key `expected_unmet_criteria`… Match the exact annotation-passing mechanism the eval solver already supports'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Match the exact annotation-passing mechanism the eval solver already supports' with explicit spec: the `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` scorer reads `case.get('expected_unmet_criteria', [])` directly from the case dict (no nested `scorer_args` wrapper), and the dataset case places `expected_unmet_criteria: [<substr1>, <substr2>]` as a top-level case key. Note that `validate_dataset_and_gold` will treat this key as a payload key (it is not in `metadata_keys`), satisfying the payload-presence check, which is acceptable here.

findings.finding: `scorer_args` is named as the per-case annotation-passing mechanism ('the case carries a scorer_args ... key expected_unmet_criteria') and the plan instructs the implementer to 'Match the exact annotation-passing mechanism the eval solver already supports; do not invent a new plumbing path.' However, `scorer_args` does not exist anywhere in the codebase: it is absent from eval.py, eval_solver.py, eval_scorers.py, all eval specs, and the metadata_keys set in validate_dataset_and_gold. There is no established `scorer_args` plumbing path to match. The implementer will not find it and the instruction 'do not invent a new plumbing path' will be contradicted by reality.
findings.location: What section — ANNOTATION SCHEMA paragraph: 'the case carries a scorer_args (or the spec's existing per-case annotation field) key expected_unmet_criteria'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove '(or the spec's existing per-case annotation field)' hedging and write: 'The case carries a top-level key `expected_unmet_criteria: [<substr1>, <substr2>]`; the scorer reads it as `case.get("expected_unmet_criteria") or []`. No wrapper key or new plumbing is needed — scorer functions already receive the full case dict.'

findings.finding: `scorer_args` does not exist anywhere in the eval infrastructure — the plan names a non-existent field as the annotation-passing mechanism, contradicting its own instruction to 'match the exact annotation-passing mechanism the eval solver already supports; do not invent a new plumbing path.'
findings.location: What section, ANNOTATION SCHEMA paragraph: 'the case carries a `scorer_args` (or the spec's existing per-case annotation field) key `expected_unmet_criteria`'
findings.suggested_fix: In the ANNOTATION SCHEMA paragraph, replace: 'the case carries a `scorer_args` (or the spec's existing per-case annotation field) key `expected_unmet_criteria`' with: 'the case carries a top-level `expected_unmet_criteria` key (alongside `id`, `expect`, `ticket_context`, `files`); the scorer reads it via `case.get("expected_unmet_criteria") or []` — the same direct case-dict access every existing scorer uses.'

findings.finding: The 'distinct finding per criterion' matching algorithm in `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` is under-specified: the plan does not state whether the matching must be bijective (each substring matched to a DIFFERENT finding, with no finding reused across multiple substrings), leaving a naive implementation that allows one finding to satisfy multiple substrings and still score 1.0.
findings.location: What section, ANNOTATION SCHEMA paragraph: 'scores 1.0 iff every substring is matched by a different finding, else 0.0'

findings.finding: The plan asserts that a 'per-case annotation field' and a `scorer_args` passing mechanism already exist in the eval solver, directing implementers to 'match the exact annotation-passing mechanism the eval solver already supports; do not invent a new plumbing path.' However, no such mechanism exists: all current scorers in `eval_scorers.py` read only the fixed case keys (`expect`, `criterion`, `kind`); `eval.py`'s `validate_dataset_and_gold` does not recognize `scorer_args` as a metadata key; and no scorer currently reads a `scorer_args` dict from a case. The plan's capability assertion is unverified — following it literally leaves the implementer with a contradiction (no existing path to match, yet forbidden from inventing one).
findings.location: What section — 'ANNOTATION SCHEMA (specify it — this is what the scorer reads): the case carries a `scorer_args` (or the spec's existing per-case annotation field) key…' and 'Match the exact annotation-passing mechanism the eval solver already supports; do not invent a new plumbing path.'
findings.suggested_fix: The simplest consistent approach: define `expected_unmet_criteria` as a plain top-level case key (like `criterion` or `kind`), add it to `metadata_keys` in `validate_dataset_and_gold` so strict validation doesn't miscount it as a payload field, and have the scorer read `case.get('expected_unmet_criteria', [])` directly — no new plumbing, fully consistent with the existing pattern.

findings.finding: The annotation-passing mechanism for `expected_unmet_criteria` is specified as '`scorer_args` (or the spec's existing per-case annotation field)' — but `scorer_args` does not exist anywhere in the eval infrastructure (zero occurrences in `src/rebar`). The established pattern (verified in `eval_scorers.py`) is for a scorer to call `case.get('<key>')` directly on the flat case dict; there is no `scorer_args` wrapper. The plan simultaneously forbids inventing a new plumbing path and names a non-existent one, leaving an executor unable to determine the correct YAML key and `case.get()` call without reverse-engineering the infrastructure.
findings.location: What section — ANNOTATION SCHEMA paragraph: 'the case carries a `scorer_args` (or the spec's existing per-case annotation field) key `expected_unmet_criteria: [...]`'
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: 'The case carries a top-level key `expected_unmet_criteria: [<substr1>, <substr2>]`. The scorer reads it as `case.get("expected_unmet_criteria") or []`. Do not wrap it in a `scorer_args` sub-dict — the eval infrastructure passes the raw case dict directly to every scorer, so top-level keys are the established pattern.'

findings.finding: `scorer_args` is presented as an 'existing per-case annotation field' that 'the eval solver already supports', but it does not exist anywhere in the codebase (zero hits in src/rebar and tests). The actual existing mechanism is plain arbitrary keys placed directly in a case dict, which the scorer function receives as its first argument (`Scorer = Callable[[dict, dict], ScoreResult]`). The plan's instruction 'Match the exact annotation-passing mechanism the eval solver already supports; do not invent a new plumbing path' rests on a named mechanism (`scorer_args`) that has no implementation to match.
findings.location: What section — 'the case carries a `scorer_args` (or the spec's existing per-case annotation field) key `expected_unmet_criteria: [<substr1>, <substr2>]` — a list of short substrings…' and 'Match the exact annotation-passing mechanism the eval solver already supports; do not invent a new plumbing path.'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, replace 'the case carries a `scorer_args` (or the spec's existing per-case annotation field) key `expected_unmet_criteria: [...]`' with: 'the case carries a top-level `expected_unmet_criteria: [<substr1>, <substr2>]` key directly in the case dict (the scorer reads it via `case.get("expected_unmet_criteria", [])` — no wrapper needed, since scorers already receive the full case dict as their first argument).' This matches the existing `Scorer = Callable[[dict, dict], ScoreResult]` contract without inventing new plumbing.

findings.finding: The plan names `scorer_args` as the annotation-passing mechanism for the `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` scorer, but no such field exists anywhere in the eval infrastructure (`eval.py`, `eval_scorers.py`, `eval_solver.py`). The actual plumbing is that every scorer receives `(case: dict, out: dict)` and reads from `case` directly — the same pattern as `_attributes_criterion` reading `case.get('criterion')`. The plan's own injunction ('Match the exact annotation-passing mechanism the eval solver already supports; do not invent a new plumbing path') contradicts naming a non-existent field.
findings.location: What section: '`scorer_args` (or the spec's existing per-case annotation field) key `expected_unmet_criteria: [<substr1>, <substr2>]`'
findings.suggested_fix: In the `CV-two-unmet-criteria` case, add `expected_unmet_criteria: ["<substr1>", "<substr2>"]` as a top-level case key (not nested under `scorer_args`). In the scorer, read it as `case.get('expected_unmet_criteria') or []`. Remove the `scorer_args` reference from the plan; the 'existing per-case annotation field' is just a top-level key on the case dict.

findings.finding: The plan uses the term 'scorer_args' as the annotation-passing mechanism, but no such field exists in the eval_solver or scorer pipeline — all existing scorers read annotation fields directly from the raw case dict; calling it 'scorer_args' may lead the implementer to invent new plumbing the plan explicitly forbids.
findings.location: What section, ANNOTATION SCHEMA paragraph: 'the case carries a scorer_args (or the spec's existing per-case annotation field) key expected_unmet_criteria'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the annotation schema sentence to: 'The CV-two-unmet-criteria case carries a flat top-level key `expected_unmet_criteria: [<substr1>, <substr2>]` in the YAML (alongside `id`, `expect`, `ticket_context`, `files`). The scorer reads it as `case.get("expected_unmet_criteria") or []` — the same pattern used by every existing scorer in eval_scorers.REGISTRY (e.g. `case.get("criterion")` in `_attributes_criterion`). No nested scorer_args envelope; no new plumbing.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'scorer_args (or the spec's existing per-case annotation field)' as the annotation-passing mechanism between the eval YAML case and the enumerates_all_unmet_criteria scorer, but scorer_args does not exist anywhere in the eval infrastructure. The actual existing mechanism is direct case-level keys (e.g., _attributes_criterion reads case.get('criterion')). The plan's parenthetical hedge signals unresolved uncertainty: an implementer following the plan might introduce a new scorer_args unpacking path (violating the plan's own 'do not invent a new plumbing path' constraint) or silently use a flat key — neither path is verified to already exist.
findings.location: What section, paragraph beginning 'ANNOTATION SCHEMA (specify it — this is what the scorer reads): the case carries a scorer_args (or the spec's existing per-case annotation field) key expected_unmet_criteria'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the hedged 'scorer_args (or the spec's existing per-case annotation field)' with a concrete decision: use a flat top-level case key expected_unmet_criteria (consistent with how _attributes_criterion reads case.get('criterion')). Update the plan's ANNOTATION SCHEMA paragraph to read: 'the case carries a top-level key expected_unmet_criteria: [<substr1>, <substr2>] — a list of short substrings the scorer reads via case.get("expected_unmet_criteria", []). This follows the existing annotation-passing pattern (plain case keys); no new plumbing is required.'

findings.finding: The plan names 'scorer_args (or the spec's existing per-case annotation field)' as the per-case annotation mechanism and states 'Match the exact annotation-passing mechanism the eval solver already supports' — but no such mechanism ('scorer_args' key, annotation envelope, or any per-case annotation field distinct from raw case keys) exists anywhere in the codebase. The actual mechanism all existing scorers use is simply case.get('key') on the raw case dict. The plan's parenthetical is ambiguous about whether the YAML key should be 'scorer_args' (a new nested key) or a top-level 'expected_unmet_criteria' key, and the assertion that it is 'already supported' plumbing is false, creating a structural gap the implementer must resolve by invention rather than by following the spec.
findings.location: What section — ANNOTATION SCHEMA paragraph: 'the case carries a scorer_args (or the spec's existing per-case annotation field) key expected_unmet_criteria: [<substr1>, <substr2>]' and 'Match the exact annotation-passing mechanism the eval solver already supports; do not invent a new plumbing path.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the parenthetical 'scorer_args (or the spec's existing per-case annotation field)' with: 'a flat top-level key `expected_unmet_criteria: [<substr1>, <substr2>]` on the case dict (consistent with how all existing scorers read per-case data: `case.get("expected_unmet_criteria") or []`).' Remove the assertion 'Match the exact annotation-passing mechanism the eval solver already supports; do not invent a new plumbing path' — this key is new and that is fine.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
